note_data.title: Manufacturing accounts
keyPoints: **Prime cost** consists of direct materials, direct labour, and direct expenses only
**Factory overhead** includes all indirect manufacturing costs such as factory rent, factory insurance, and depreciation of plant and machinery
**Production cost** equals prime cost plus factory overhead (production cost = prime cost + factory overhead)
**Work in progress** represents partly finished goods at the beginning and end of the accounting period
**Factory cost of goods produced** equals production cost plus opening work in progress minus closing work in progress
**Direct materials** equals opening stock plus purchases minus closing stock of raw materials
Manufacturing accounts are prepared to calculate the **cost of goods produced** which is then transferred to the trading account
Factory overhead is **absorbed** into production cost and distributed over all units produced during the period

definitions.definition: The total of direct materials, direct labour and direct expenses incurred in manufacturing
All indirect costs incurred in the factory during production such as factory rent, factory insurance and depreciation of plant and machinery
Prime cost plus factory overhead representing the total cost of manufacturing goods
Partly finished goods that are still in the process of manufacture at the end of the accounting period
Raw materials that can be directly identified with and allocated to specific units of production

howToAnswer.instruction: Show all workings step by step. Use the manufacturing account format with clear headings.
Draw up the full manufacturing account with all three sections: prime cost, factory overhead, and work in progress adjustments.
Give the formula or component without explanation, e.g., 'Prime cost = direct materials + direct labour + direct expenses'

modelAnswer.question: Karachi Textiles Ltd has the following information for the year ended 31 December 2023: Opening stock of raw materials $15,000; Purchases of raw materials $85,000; Closing stock of raw materials $12,000; Direct labour $45,000; Factory rent $8,000. Calculate the prime cost.
modelAnswer.answer: Direct materials = Opening stock + Purchases - Closing stock = $15,000 + $85,000 - $12,000 = $88,000. Prime cost = Direct materials + Direct labour = $88,000 + $45,000 = $133,000.
markBreakdown: Mark 2: Prime cost formula applied correctly

commonMistakes: Never include factory overhead in prime cost — prime cost only includes direct costs
Students often forget to adjust for opening and closing work in progress when calculating factory cost of goods produced
Never include selling and distribution expenses in manufacturing accounts — these belong in profit and loss account
Students confuse direct and indirect costs — factory supervisor salary is indirect, machine operator salary is direct
Never add closing work in progress — it should be deducted to find factory cost of goods produced
examinerTips: Manufacturing accounts appear frequently in Paper 2 Section B
Always use the three-section format: Prime cost, Factory overhead, Work in progress adjustments
Show all calculations separately before entering into the manufacturing account
The figure for factory cost of goods produced transfers to the trading account as purchases
formulas: Prime cost = Direct materials + Direct labour + Direct expenses
Direct materials = Opening stock + Purchases - Closing stock
Production cost = Prime cost + Factory overhead
Factory cost of goods produced = Production cost + Opening WIP - Closing WIP
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Manufacturing Account with three distinct sections: Prime Cost section (listing direct materials, direct labour, direct expenses), Factory Overhead section (listing all indirect factory costs), and Work in Progress section (showing opening WIP added and closing WIP deducted to arrive at Factory Cost of Goods Produced)
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P2 Q4 (8 marks) — they asked students to prepare a manufacturing account from a trial balance including raw materials consumed, direct labor, and factory overheads
O/N 2022 P2 Q3 (6 marks) tested calculation of prime cost and factory cost when given opening/closing work-in-progress figures
M/J 2022 P2 Q5 (10 marks) required a complete manufacturing account with adjustments for depreciation of factory equipment and accrued factory wages
O/N 2021 P2 Q4 (7 marks) focused on distinguishing between factory overheads and administrative expenses in manufacturing account preparation

workedExample.question: ABC Manufacturing Ltd has the following information for the year ended 31 December 2023: Raw materials at start $8,000, Raw materials purchased $45,000, Raw materials at end $6,000, Direct wages $25,000, Factory rent $12,000, Factory insurance $3,000, Work-in-progress at start $4,000, Work-in-progress at end $7,000. Prepare the manufacturing account. (8 marks)
steps: Step 1: Calculate raw materials consumed = Opening stock + Purchases - Closing stock = $8,000 + $45,000 - $6,000 = $47,000
Step 2: Calculate prime cost = Raw materials consumed + Direct wages = $47,000 + $25,000 = $72,000
Step 3: Calculate factory overheads = Factory rent + Factory insurance = $12,000 + $3,000 = $15,000
Step 4: Calculate factory cost of production = Prime cost + Factory overheads + Opening WIP - Closing WIP = $72,000 + $15,000 + $4,000 - $7,000 = $84,000
workedExample.answer:
ABC Manufacturing Ltd - Manufacturing Account for year ended 31 December 2023:
Raw materials consumed $47,000
Direct wages $25,000
Prime cost $72,000
Factory overheads $15,000
Work-in-progress (opening) $4,000
Less: Work-in-progress (closing) ($7,000)
Factory cost of production $84,000
workedExample.markScheme: 2 marks for correct raw materials consumed calculation, 1 mark for prime cost, 2 marks for factory overheads identification, 2 marks for work-in-progress adjustment, 1 mark for final factory cost figure

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates confused factory overheads with administrative expenses' — always classify expenses carefully, ensuring factory-related costs like factory rent and factory insurance are included in manufacturing account, not trading account
Examiner Report 2022: 'Students frequently forgot to adjust for work-in-progress' — always add opening WIP and subtract closing WIP to arrive at the correct factory cost of production

Examiner Report 2021: 'Poor presentation of manufacturing account format' — always use proper headings, show prime cost subtotal clearly, and transfer final figure to trading account as cost of goods manufactured
note_data.keyDiagram: Students must be able to draw the manufacturing account format showing three distinct sections: (1) Raw Materials section with opening stock + purchases - closing stock = consumed, (2) Prime Cost section adding raw materials consumed + direct labor + direct expenses, (3) Factory Cost section adding prime cost + factory overheads + opening WIP - closing WIP = factory cost of production. The diagram should show clear arrows indicating the flow and transfer of the final figure to the trading account as 'Cost of Goods Manufactured'.
note_data.quickRecap: Manufacturing accounts calculate the cost of producing goods in a factory. Start with raw materials consumed (opening + purchases - closing), add direct wages for prime cost, then add factory overheads and adjust for work-in-progress. The final factory cost of production transfers to the trading account. Remember: only include factory-related expenses, not administrative costs.
